#PowerView and related share-discovery helpers such as Invoke-ShareFinder or Find-DomainShare can map remote SMB shares across a domain and optionally test whether the current user can read them. This turns broad endpoint discovery into a concrete map of reachable UNC paths, helping the attacker identify locations where policy backups, scripts, documents, or legacy secrets can be collected immediately. The runbook also demonstrates reading discovered share content such as old GPP-related files from SYSVOL.

  1. How attack works: The attacker enumerates domain computers and sweeps them for readable shares.
  2. How attack works: The current credential context is tested against remote SMB shares and only accessible paths are returned.
  3. How attack works: Interesting share content is browsed and can reveal follow-on secrets such as old GPP data.
  • Tool Location/Source: PowerView is part of the PowerSploit framework.
  • Attack pre-requesite: A PowerShell session with network reachability to remote SMB endpoints and credentials that can read one or more shares.
  • What attacker gains: A map of accessible UNC paths and potentially direct access to valuable data stored on those shares.
  • Detection/Defense opsec: Monitor broad share-enumeration sweeps, restrict unnecessary share exposure, and investigate large-scale UNC path probing from user workstations.

label: Password Spraying (PowerShell)
command:
# very first step, identify password lockout thresholds:

PS > net accounts 

# then 

PS > powershell -ep bypass

PS > .\Spray-Passwords.ps1 -Pass Nexus123! -Admin
description:
Password spraying attacks many accounts with a single likely password rather than hammering one account repeatedly. This runbook starts by checking lockout policy, then runs a PowerShell spraying script with a chosen password candidate across many domain users. When executed carefully, spraying can reveal weak credentials while reducing the chance of triggering account lockouts compared with traditional brute-force attacks.

  1. How attack works: The attacker reviews lockout policy to understand the safe spray threshold.
  2. How attack works: A single password candidate is tested horizontally across many domain accounts.
  3. How attack works: Any successful authentications reveal valid AD credentials for later access.
  • Tool Location/Source: The workflow uses native PowerShell plus a password-spraying script such as Spray-Passwords.ps1.
  • Attack pre-requesite: A candidate password, a reachable Domain Controller or auth path, and enough knowledge of lockout policy to avoid mass account disruption.
  • What attacker gains: Valid domain credentials for accounts that reuse weak or predictable passwords.
  • Detection/Defense opsec: Monitor distributed failed logons against many accounts, enforce MFA and strong password policy, and alert on horizontal authentication patterns from a single host.

label: AS-REP Roasting (Rubeus)
command: .\Rubeus.exe asreproast /nowrap
description:
#Rubeus can perform AS-REP Roasting directly from a compromised Windows host by enumerating users with preauthentication disabled and requesting roastable Kerberos material in memory. Unlike the Impacket variant, this runbook does not need a separate user list in the command shown; Rubeus can automate the discovery and retrieval flow from the current domain context. The resulting hashes can then be cracked offline to recover plaintext passwords for misconfigured accounts.

  1. How attack works: The attacker loads or executes #Rubeus from the compromised Windows endpoint.
  2. How attack works: Rubeus queries the domain and requests roastable AS-REP material for accounts with preauthentication disabled.
  3. How attack works: The returned hashes are exported for later cracking or credential abuse.
  • Tool Location/Source: #Rubeus is available from the GhostPack Rubeus project.
  • Attack pre-requesite: A domain-aware Windows foothold and one or more users with Kerberos preauthentication disabled.
  • What attacker gains: Crackable AS-REP hashes for vulnerable accounts without needing the target passwords in advance.
  • Detection/Defense opsec: Disable preauth exceptions, monitor unusual AS-REQ behavior from workstations, and inspect in-memory .NET tooling or execute-assembly workflows that invoke Rubeus.

label: Kerberoasting (Rubeus)
command: .\Rubeus.exe kerberoast /outfile:hashes.kerberoast
description:
#Rubeus can perform Kerberoasting directly from a compromised Windows host by enumerating SPN-bearing accounts and requesting service tickets that are crackable offline. Because the ticket material is returned in a format that can be saved to disk or piped elsewhere, the operator can combine on-host enumeration with later offline cracking. This is a common Windows-native alternative to Impacket-based Kerberoasting when the attacker already has a domain foothold on a Windows endpoint.

  1. How attack works: The attacker executes Rubeus from the compromised endpoint.
  2. How attack works: Rubeus identifies SPN-bearing accounts and requests Kerberos service tickets for them.
  3. How attack works: The resulting Kerberoast hashes are exported for later cracking.
  • Tool Location/Source: #Rubeus is available from the GhostPack Rubeus project.
  • Attack pre-requesite: A Windows foothold in the target domain and the ability to request service tickets from the KDC.
  • What attacker gains: Crackable service-ticket material for SPN-bearing accounts, often tied to service identities with elevated access.
  • Detection/Defense opsec: Monitor spikes in TGS requests, use strong service-account passwords or gMSAs, and watch for in-memory Rubeus execution on endpoints.

label: Silver Ticket (Mimikatz)
command:
# check first if current user has permission to service using PS iwr utility

PS > iwr -UseDefaultCredentials http://web04

# if no, fire mimikatz and dump the NTLM Hash of the service (imagine its hard) : 

mimikatz# privilege::debug
mimikatz# sekurlsa::logonpasswords

# get also  user Domain SID

whoami /user

# SPN you know it already for service.. then time to generate the TGS

mimikatz # kerberos::golden /sid:S-1-5-21-1987370270-658905905-1781884369 /domain:corp.com /ptt /target:web04.corp.com /service:http /rc4:4d28cf5252d39971419580a51484ca09 /user:jeffadmin

# exit mimikatz, and check list
klist

# now check you have access :

PS > iwr -UseDefaultCredentials http://web04
description:
A Silver Ticket attack forges a service ticket (TGS) for a specific service rather than a TGT for the entire domain. With #Mimikatz, the attacker uses the target service account's secret, the domain SID, and the service SPN to create a ticket that the service will trust directly. Unlike a Golden Ticket, the KDC can be bypassed once the forged service ticket is injected, which makes Silver Tickets useful for targeted persistence or service-specific lateral movement when the service account secret is known.

  1. How attack works: The attacker gathers the service account hash, domain SID, and target SPN.
  2. How attack works: Mimikatz forges a service-specific Kerberos ticket offline and injects it into the session.
  3. How attack works: The forged ticket is used directly against the target service for privileged access.
  • Tool Location/Source: #Mimikatz is available from the Mimikatz project.
  • Attack pre-requesite: Knowledge of the target SPN, the relevant service account or computer-account secret, and the domain SID.
  • What attacker gains: Service-specific Kerberos access that can bypass normal KDC-mediated validation for that target service.
  • Detection/Defense opsec: Monitor unusual Kerberos service-ticket behavior, protect service account secrets, and investigate access that appears valid to a service but lacks a normal KDC-driven history.

label: DCSync (Mimikatz)
command:
mimikaz # lsadump::dcsync /user:corp\dave

hashcat -m 1000 hashes.dcsync /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt -r
/usr/share/hashcat/rules/best64.rule --force
description:
#Mimikatz can perform DCSync by invoking the domain replication interfaces to request password data for a chosen account, such as krbtgt or another privileged principal. Instead of extracting NTDS.dit from disk, the attacker impersonates a legitimate replication partner and asks the Domain Controller to replicate credential material over the network. This makes DCSync one of the highest-impact credential-access techniques in Active Directory when the attacker has the necessary replication rights.

  1. How attack works: The attacker runs lsadump::dcsync from Mimikatz with a target user.
  2. How attack works: The Domain Controller returns replicated credential material for that user.
  3. How attack works: The resulting hash or key material is reused for follow-on attacks such as ticket forgery or pass-the-hash.
  • Tool Location/Source: #Mimikatz provides lsadump::dcsync for replication abuse.
  • Attack pre-requesite: Rights such as Replicating Directory Changes and related permissions, plus network reachability to the Domain Controller.
  • What attacker gains: NTLM and key material for selected domain accounts without touching the database files directly.
  • Detection/Defense opsec: Audit replication rights, monitor DRSUAPI activity from non-DC systems, and alert on DCSync behavior targeting high-value accounts.

label: DCSync (secretsdump)
command:
impacket-secretsdump -just-dc-user dave
corp.com/jeffadmin:"BrouhahaTungPerorateBroom2023\!"@192.168.50.70
description:
#Impacket's secretsdump can perform DCSync remotely from a Linux attacker system by binding to the same directory replication interfaces used by Domain Controllers. Instead of running Windows tooling on a compromised host, the attacker uses valid domain credentials with replication rights and asks the Domain Controller to return account credential material over the network. This makes secretsdump a flexible remote option for high-impact AD credential theft.

  1. How attack works: The attacker launches impacket-secretsdump with replication-capable credentials.
  2. How attack works: The tool binds to the replication interface and requests the targeted account's credential material.
  3. How attack works: Returned hashes or keys are saved for follow-on abuse such as pass-the-hash or ticket forgery.
  • Tool Location/Source: #Impacket provides secretsdump for remote DCSync-style operations.
  • Attack pre-requesite: Credentials with replication rights and reachability to the Domain Controller over the required SMB/RPC/DCERPC paths.
  • What attacker gains: Domain hashes or key material for selected users without needing on-host database access.
  • Detection/Defense opsec: Alert on replication behavior from non-DC hosts, audit replication-right assignments, and monitor remote secretsdump-like DCSync activity.
